title: Sanding a wooden floor by machine
subtitle: Belt sander for the field, edge sander for the perimeter, hand sanding for the corners
excerpt: How to sand and re-finish a solid or engineered hardwood floor using a drum belt sander and edge sander: setting the sanding sequence, working through the grits, filling any gaps, and applying the finish coats.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. BS 8201 Code of practice for the installation of flooring. Wood Floor Association technical guidance (publicly available).
aliases: how to sand and restore a wooden floor

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating floor finish combining natural waxes and drying oils. It soaks into the wood rather than forming a surface film, giving a more natural appearance than lacquer. Spot-repairable: damaged sections can be re-oiled without stripping the whole floor.

content.text: A solid hardwood floor can be sanded and re-finished many times in its life; the floor is typically thick enough to carry four to six complete sand-and-finish cycles. Engineered floors can usually be sanded once or twice, depending on the thickness of the hardwood wear layer. The sanding work divides into three areas: the field (the main area, covered by the drum belt sander), the perimeter (covered by a rotary edge sander), and the corners and doorways (covered by hand with a sanding block).
Step 1, prepare the floor and room
Punch all nail heads below the surface.
A raised nail head will tear the sanding belt. Set every nail at least 3 mm below the wood surface with a nail punch.
Seal the room.
Floor sanding produces enormous quantities of fine dust. Tape dust sheets over doorways and remove or cover soft furnishings. Open windows if available.
Hire the machines.

A drum belt sander and an edge sander. Available from most hire shops by the day. The hire shop will supply the correct belt papers for the machine; buy at least 10 belts for the drum and 20 discs for the edge sander (the papers clog and wear faster than expected).
Sanding sequence
Step 1, first pass with 40-grit.

Start with 40-grit on the drum sander. Work diagonally across the boards at 45 degrees (not parallel to the boards) for the first pass. The diagonal direction cuts through old finish and surface irregularities faster than working along the grain. Keep the machine moving at all times; a stationary drum sander removes wood in seconds.
Step 2, second pass at 40-grit.
Sand diagonally in the opposite direction (the other 45-degree angle) to remove the cross-hatch pattern from the first pass.
Step 3, edge sander at 40-grit.
Sand the perimeter strip the drum sander cannot reach with the edge sander at the same grit. Work in the direction of the grain where possible.
Step 4, fill gaps.

Sweep up and vacuum thoroughly. Mix fine sawdust from the dust bag with wood floor filler to a stiff paste. Press it into any gaps or cracks with the 100 mm filling knife. Allow to dry fully before proceeding with the next grit.
Steps 5 to 7, progress through 80-grit and 120-grit.

Repeat the drum sanding, now working along the grain (parallel to the boards), at 80-grit and then 120-grit. Edge-sand at each stage. By the 120-grit pass the surface should be smooth and consistent across the full floor. Vacuum between every pass to prevent coarser grit from the floor contaminating the sanding paper.
Finishing
Step 8, apply the first finish coat.
Vacuum the floor completely and remove all dust with a tack cloth. Apply the finish, working away from the door in the direction of the grain.
Hard-wax oil

is the most forgiving finish for first-time floor finishing; it is applied by brush or cloth, soaks in rather than pooling, and gives a matte or satin result. Lacquer is more durable but less forgiving of application errors.

Allow the first coat to cure fully per the product data sheet (typically 12 to 24 hours). Lightly denib with 180-grit on the sanding block, vacuum, and apply the second coat. Most hard-wax oils require two coats; some require three.
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: The machine was left stationary briefly, sanding a hollow into the floor.
attrs.fix: The hollow needs to be sanded out by hand with 80-grit before finishing. Sand the surrounding boards with 120-grit to blend the repair, vacuum, and refinish.

attrs.symptom: Darker circle or band at the perimeter from the edge sander
attrs.cause: The edge sander was used at a coarser grit than the final drum-sander pass, leaving a different surface texture that absorbs finish differently.
attrs.fix: Go back with the edge sander at the same final grit as the drum pass. Vacuum and refinish.

attrs.cause: Finish applied over dust, or over a surface that was not fully dry from the previous coat.
attrs.fix: Allow the finish to cure fully. Lightly sand with 180-grit, vacuum completely, and re-apply the finish coat to the affected section.
